speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us how you registered to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I received the answer. I received a letter that I was accepted. And in April of 1939, I left Krefeld. Left everyone, everything behind. And with 11 suitcases and a bicycle, I went first to Holland, and from there to Rotterdam, and from Rotterdam to England. And there I was picked up by the-- oh, well, first I have to explain how I got out with a-- a certificate that said born in 1920 and a passport that said born in 1921. And the fear that I-- that would be discovered was great. I was very fortunate, because we lived so close to the Dutch border that by the time we-- we arrived in Holland, in Venlo, we-- the-- the official person who-- who the-- controls-- I mean, checks all the-- the documents, was in the department right next to me. I heard his voice, and shortly-- and I was-- was very scary. And then I heard outside yelling, [SPEAKING GERMAN], that the Dutch couldn't wait to get these Germans off the train. So they never came to my-- to my compartment. And I was safe there. But then came the danger of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back for--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they check it--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a moment, OK. I would like you to tell us what happened when you finally told your parents that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I then-- I then went to visit my father in prison. He was in prison in Krefeld. And I had to tell them there what I did and that I was accepted. And I said goodbye. And it was very difficult, one of the hardest things I ever did. He felt totally unable to be of any support to his family, to me. And was equally difficult, then, to say goodbye to him. I went to see my grandparents. And I was still so young. And it was all so-- everything came so very fast. And so I went on the train, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't even come to the station. I told them not to. Our chauffeur was there. Aretz was there with his daughter, and a few other people. I asked them not to come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you prepare for the trip, packing--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother. And we had to pack and we had to-- it was all the strictest control by the Germans. And we made [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that moment when you told your mother that you're going to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we were-- that I was leaving? No. My mother had a very severe nervous breakdown after this first-- after the Kristallnacht, and then when they took my father. And she kept screaming all the time, which I told you we wanted-- I want us all to kill-- kill ourselves. She wanted, really, to commit suicide. And I screamed just as hard, don't say that. We must continue, and we'll make it, and we'll-- we'll-- [INAUDIBLE] will come home again, and-- so I-- I guess she was able to accept anything, anything. For me to leave and promise I will do everything in my power to get you out, to save your life. And I was also thinking of my grandparents, of course. And I had one cousin, Hans, who was exactly my age, also 17. And I wanted them all to be saved. I knew there was no hope in Germany anymore. I knew that. I was fully aware of it. I was-- I didn't care how many of my forefathers-- 1462, I think, goes back my generation in-- in that particular area in Krefeld. I couldn't have cared less at that time. I wanted out. And they accepted. My mother accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the train started to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was just plain-- I don't think there was anything. I was-- if-- I felt like a stone, like there was no-- nothing inside of me. I just said goodbye to everything I ever knew. I said goodbye to-- to not only the people, but all the things, all the-- all my possessions, everything was left-- it was behind me, my whole life. My previous life was-- I had no idea what was going to happen. I went to a country that-- where I didn't speak their language. They didn't speak my language. I left with 5 marks or 10 marks or something in my pocket. I had no money. And I had-- I was on my own, completely. But I do remember the-- when I heard-- when I was in Holland, this enormous-- this deep breath that I'm free now. My-- I have a new life starting, and I'll do the best I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you arrived to London, to-- to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: England? I was picked up by the-- it was a farm. I didn't even know exactly where I was going. And there I sat with 11 suitcases around me. My mother sent the-- she had the-- everything had to be washed, you know. The-- the-- everything was wet, and-- and so my mother had all the-- all the table cloth and linens. And she gave it to me. She said, that will be for your dowry. At least you will have that out. So my suitcases were all packed. And there I sat with 11 suitcases.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the farmer came, Mr. Sanderson came, stood there, and said which one is yours? And I said, all of them. He almost-- well, he was very surprised. He said, I have a little Ford two seater. There's just enough room for one suitcase in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we had to rent a-- a truck. And they brought all of this the following day. Somebody brought all the-- all the equipment. And then I went to a life that was unimaginable for me before I got there. Big farm, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frinton-on-Sea, on a-- on a little hill called Great Holland Hall, next to a church and a cemetery. And there was nothing else. And there I was. When I walked into the kitchen, the half of pigs were-- were on-- hanging from the ceiling. And no heat anywhere, just fireplaces in every room. And then I was told what I had to do. And their little girl, Jill, she was two years old. And from the second I laid eyes on her, I was in love with her, and we-- she with me. And that was my only consolation that whole time in that house. Was enormous. The kitchen was I think as big as my whole house. I had to scrub it every day on my hands and knees. It was very strict. I had to get up very early. And sometimes after it was all finished with the-- with cleaning the house, and the kitchen, and the hearth had to be made with the white on the bottom, and the stove was one of those old fashioned ones, black on the top. And I had to do all that. And sometimes the farmer came in, if there was a cow was calving or whatever, or a baby horse was born. He came in with his dirty shoes and went right through the kitchen. And I don't-- didn't matter what time of the day or night it was, I had to clean that kitchen again. It was hard. It was hard work. And I was lonely, very lonely. And I remember distinctly one-- after I was there a little while, and I could speak, understand a little bit more English, how this farmer's wife, Mrs. Sanderson, said on the telephone, you know, I have a new girl here. She's Jewish, but she is very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it kind of got me. Why did she say but? And I said, my-- am I back in Germany again? What is going on? So it was a new life, very new. How did they treat you in general, the Sandersons?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very egocentric, hysterical person, the woman. He was nice. He was very gentle, very understanding. She-- you-- I could-- I never knew the next moment how she would behave towards me. Then after the war, they got divorced immediately. And I never-- I could understand why. So it wasn't an easy life. But the little girl, she was everything to me. And she-- and we still know her to this day. And we see her. We have seen her several times. She has-- they have visited here. So it was a wonderful relationship. Oh, and then, when the war started, we had four-- I had an addition of-- of five members, four teenage girls from London who were evacuees, with their teacher. So instead of two adults and a child, I had seven adults and a child, and I had to cook and clean and do all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war started, what went through your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing that went through my head is what's going to happen to-- to my family? My parents, by that time, I must say, 10 days before the war started, my parents arrived in England. Why? Because as I had promised, I was able to get them to England. I went to the Bloomsbury House which was the HIAS organization in London. And I applied. I was already in England, and it helped that I was there. And I applied just for my parents and sister. That's all they gave me permission for. And then they told me-- and I filled out the application, and when I brought it back to the man to the-- in the office, the clerk, he said, put it in this basket, we'll take care of it. And I said, no, you won't. I said, I want the signature today. I'm not leaving until everything is signed and I know that it will go straight through to Germany. My father was-- was promised that if my-- if he could leave Germany within 24 hours, that he would be able to leave. I wanted to further this and to allow this to materialize. They wouldn't do it. They wouldn't do it. I said, I'm not leaving. They were-- still said, this is-- this is the-- you know, they were also former Germans. They all spoke German. And they-- they were still very German about everything. I-- so I sat down on the floor, on the-- in the outside, on the-- in the waiting hall, a cold, marble floor. And-- and there I sat. And then it was 5 o'clock, and they were ready to close. And they came out, and they were going to-- and they told me to leave. And I said, I'm not leaving. And I said, you know, if you try to get me out, I'll scream so loud that every newspaper in England is going to write about it. I want this permit today. And I got it. I got it. They did it. They wanted this passed out of there. And 10 days before the war started, my parents were able to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with them? During--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents? Yes. I was able to write, and they wrote back. We even were able to talk to each other two or three times over the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when they arrived to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- still with the farmer. And they arrived in-- they went to Slough, where I-- we had a relatives, close friends of my parents. And they rented an apartment. And they lived there. And they wanted-- and I wanted to go to them, because when the-- after the war, 10 days later, the war started. And I felt if the Germans come and-- I-- we-- I want to go. We want to-- want to go all together if-- you know, if they bomb us. And Mrs. Sanderson wouldn't let me go. And I was committed to stay there. I was not allowed to leave until they allowed it. She would not allow it. She wouldn't let me go. And that made things even more difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their house? Nine months. And then, finally, they had no choice. I-- I-- I think there was a limit. You have to stay in the area for-- for so long. And then, finally, I said, I will stay here until you get someone else. And I will teach her what I know. And I did that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went to Slough to live with my parents and my sister. And my sister-- we-- my sister and I went to school. And we learned-- tried to learn shorthand and typing and English. By that time I spoke English pretty well, but I spoke it was a-- English-- English with a-- I had French. I spoke fluent French before, with a French and German accent. And I was pretty messy. When I came to America and I tried to speak American English, it was even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father and mother do in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- they also went to school to learn English. And other than that, he-- nobody was allowed to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents had sent a lift, which is a huge box, like as big as a room, with furniture, one to England, and one to America. And we-- when the war started, we were told now all the numbers of the people who are still in Germany are exhausted, are finished. They will never be called, which means that every quota number outside of Germany had moved forward. It was a terrible thought, that we are taking over for those who were not able to go. But for-- for-- for us, it was fortunate. But I never forgot the fact that the numbers that we were given, that we were able to leave and go to America while the people who couldn't were probably killed. Very hard to accept.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in England, did you experience any air raids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my goodness, yes. Dozens and dozens and dozens. It kept-- I didn't even go anymore, because when I was on the farm, they had ditches. And we didn't have air raid shelters there. And we had to go-- we all had boots. And we had to go into these wet ditches. And I realized that the best time for me to write my diary was when they all went into the ditches. I stayed in the house. I didn't even go anymore. It was one after the other. And the news got worse and worse and worse. And-- and I tried, at one time after the war started, they said that the Red Cross was helping to get some Jews out, especially young people. And I tried to get my cousin Hans adopted by someone. There-- I met a-- I met a person who-- who had offered to adopt me, offered to adopt me. And I said no. This was even before my parents came. I said my parents-- I-- I'm sure they are going to be here before the war starts-- started-- would start.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, would you adopt my cousin instead? And they-- they said yes. So I had everything arranged. The Red Cross, everything was sent to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two days later, the war started, and my cousin never found out that I tried to save his life. He perished. We don't have-- he and his mother perished, together with the rest. My grandmother committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to pick her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- we had a doctor in Krefeld, a-- our pediatrician. Doctor Herschfelder, who vowed that he would not leave until every Jew was safe. So he was still in Krefeld. And he gave every surviving Jew, everyone who was still living there, a cyanide pill. And my grandmother used it. To my knowledge she was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of the grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- they were all-- there were all taken to Theresienstadt and they were all killed. And my aunt and cousin also, and almost all of the cousins of my-- of my grandparents. And very few people survived. We-- I had no one except for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your grandmother committed suicide, did you know about it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know that the other grandparents and the other relatives are taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea. We were hoping, even after the war was over and we heard what happened, and of-- but we know everything. We know all the detail, because our chauffeur and his son went over the wall. They couldn't-- there was nothing to eat for my grandparents anymore. So his son, every single day, went over the wall with sandwiches and kept them alive with food. And that boy was sent because everybody knew about Aretz, what he did for us. And he was beaten so many times. And the boy was sent as cannon fodder. And on the very first day of the war, he was already killed. And when his mother found out about it, she committed suicide. It was a horror. We heard all that after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the chauffeur still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he alive--

speaker: interviewee
text: --died from cancer shortly after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never met him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents meet the Sandersons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when they came to England they came almost right away to-- to Frinton-on-Sea. They met and the-- the farm-- farmer family was very impressed with them. My parents were-- they-- they thought they were wonderful people. And-- but then was-- everything was so expensive. So we couldn't go back and forth. And only once in a while were we-- could we call each other on the telephone. But they did meet, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The little girl, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in touch with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went back-- my husband and I went back to England, 1968. And we went to Frinton, Frinton-on-Sea, and we met the family. Mrs.-- they were all still alive. Mr. Sanderson had remarried, had two sons from the second marriage. And I-- we-- we met Jill and her husband. And we've been friends ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost a year and a half, not quite.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went where after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States. We got--

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: --our-- in May of 1940. No. '39. 1939. The war started the end of 39. And we went-- the war-- what's the matter with me? 1939. No, 19-- what-- the war started in '39. And we-- in May-- in November. 1940. 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an aunt, a-- her husband was a cousin of my mother's, and-- but they were the very closest friends. Very closest friends. And she had a brother who was an American. And they were in Chicago first, and they set it up for us. We sent-- she used our furniture that was sent there, and then she set up an apartment for us. And we went. We lived in Chicago with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived the end of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, Well, first in New York, and then we went to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a ship called the-- it was the Cunard line, and it was called the Scythia. They brought us over safely. They went back and took on another load of refugees, and were sunk by a mine. Nobody survived. And we made it to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you came to the harbor and when you first saw the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being too-- I was totally exhausted from all the past and that this really was happening. And on top of it, there were 150 soldiers, Canadian soldiers and sailors, on the ship. And I had a-- really a wonderful time. I was one of the few that didn't-- wasn't seasick. And to see the Statue of Liberty, I think I stood out there and I said, I'm glad to meet you. Not this euphoria, none of that. I knew what was ahead. We-- I was full of-- of trepidation. I-- when I hear people making all this-- no, I didn't have that. I was very calm and collected, realizing this is going to be a very different life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what the Statue of Liberty stood for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only knew that all the grownups were always so excited about it. And it had something to do with freedom. But I-- what did I know about freedom? I really don't think I had a concept of what freedom really meant, especially since I didn't find it that exciting, that obvious in England. Like I told you, this-- the way she said, but she is nice, this seemed to come up more than once. This is my-- it was always the fact that I want you to meet my new Jewish maid. Always the word Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she have to put that in? I didn't realize it then but it did-- there was something there that was wrong. I was just a girl who came, a refugee, but why always this word Jewish? We have to change tapes again.
